title: The Reef of Kamehameha
blurb: A prince masquerading as a rebel surfer. A kingdom stolen in the night. One month to prove his bloodline—or lose everything. Betrayal lurks in every barrel.
comparable: For fans of *The Lies of Locke Lamora* by Scott Lynch, *Red, White & Royal Blue* by Casey McQuiston

chapters.text:
Chapter 1

The North Shore swallowed sound as the first wave of the set crested over Ehukai Beach. Sunlight fractured across the water, turning the Pacific into a sheet of hammered bronze. Lono Kapule adjusted the grip on his gun-metal gray board, his fingers tracing the worn rail where years of wipeouts had sanded the fiberglass smooth. He didn’t check the lip of the wave—not yet. He didn’t need to. He knew Pipe. Better than any man alive.

A voice cut through the wind. “Last heat before the wildcard cutoff. You gonna make it look easy or just kill yourself trying?”

Lono turned. Jax Dawson stood waist-deep in the shorebreak, arms crossed, board tucked under his arm like a soldier’s rifle. The two men had split waves their entire lives—sometimes as friends, more often as rivals. Today, there was no mistaking the smirk on Dawson’s face. He wanted Lono to crack.

Lono spat a mouthful of seawater. “I’m gonna make it look like nothing, brother. Like you’re watching a ghost ride.”

Dawson laughed, but it didn’t reach his eyes. “Ghosts don’t have sponsors. Ghosts don’t have futures.”

“Then I guess they’re lucky.”

The swell deepened. A roar built behind them. The lineup waited. Lono didn’t wait for Dawson to move. He dropped the board to the water and pushed off, paddling hard, shoulders burning. Salt stung his lips. The wave grew behind him, a blue-green wall rising like a cathedral door. He didn’t look back.

“You’re kidding yourself!” Dawson called after him. “Tourists ride Pipe to die. Legends ride it to dance.”

Lono didn’t answer. He tucked into a push-up, felt the board lift. The wave lifted him. The world narrowed to the curl of froth and the hollow roar of the ocean sucking breath out of everything.

He stood. Water drained off his legs. The board flew beneath him, a silver bullet skimming the lip of a liquid tunnel. He carved across the face, spray exploding like gunfire. The crowd on the shore erupted—more shock than awe. Lono Kapule wasn’t supposed to ride like this. He rode like a man who had nothing to lose.

Kai Ulu lifted his camera, fingers clicking rapid-fire. He caught the way Lono’s hips led the turn, the way his left hand feathered the rail, carving a perfect arc that made the wave sing. Kai had seen Lono shred hundreds of swells, but today felt different. Today felt electric. The way Lono moved—it wasn’t just skill. It was memory. It was blood.

“That’s not a drifter,” said a woman beside Kai, her voice tight with envy. “That’s a god.”

Kai lowered the camera for a second. He knew the truth. The world didn’t.

The wave peaked. Lono launched off the lip, flying into the air, board flipping beneath him in a reverse snap. For a heartbeat, he hung suspended—weightless, weighty, alive. Then he landed, knee-deep in the flats, still gliding forward as the wave dissipated into foam.

The beach erupted. Boards clattered on rock. Shouts rose—“Lucky bastard!” “Should’ve been a wipeout!” “No way that’s real!”

Dawson shook his head as he paddled in. “Unbelievable. He’s either the luckiest man on the planet or the most reckless.”

Kai kept shooting. He zoomed in on Lono’s face as he waded out, the salt and sun turning his skin bronze, his dark eyes unreadable. Kai knew that look. It wasn’t joy. It was war.

“He rides like he’s fighting someone,” Kai muttered.

“Or something,” said the woman beside him, her voice soft.

Lono dropped his board in the sand and grabbed a towel. He wiped his face, then his arms, never taking his eyes off the horizon. The next set was already building. He could feel it in his bones—the call of the ocean, the whisper of destiny in every swell.

“You’re up,” Dawson said, passing by. “Try not to drown.”

“Can’t promise anything,” Lono replied.

Dawson smirked, but there was no warmth in it. “You’re not a local. You’re not a legend. You’re just another haole with a death wish.”

Lono turned slowly. He knew Dawson didn’t mean it. Not really. But words cut deeper when they were almost true.

“Maybe,” Lono said. “Or maybe I’m the only one who sees what’s really out there.”

Dawson scoffed and walked off, but Lono didn’t watch him go. His gaze drifted to the water, where a lone figure in a crimson wetsuit was paddling out toward the lineup. She moved with a quiet confidence, her strokes long and purposeful. No wasted motion. No fear.

Sophie Laurent.

She wasn’t supposed to be here. Not yet. Not in this heat. The wildcard qualifier was restricted to invited riders only. But rules had never stopped Sophie before. She was heir to Laurent Surfboards, the French empire that had tried to buy Pipe three years running. She wore the red wetsuit like a flag—defiance, privilege, ambition all tangled together.

Lono felt something tighten in his chest. Not admiration. Not kindness. Something older. Something like recognition.

She caught the next wave. Not the biggest. Not the most perfect. But she rode it with a precision that made the crowd hush. She bottom-turned sharp, carving upward like she was drawing a line in the water. The wave responded as if it knew her.

Lono didn’t realize he was walking toward the water until the sand gave way under his feet. Kai followed, camera still in hand.

“You know her?” Kai asked.

“Everyone knows her,” Lono said. “But I know how she rides.”

“And how’s that?”

Lono looked at him then. Really looked. Kai’s hands were steady, but his eyes betrayed him—wide, alert. He knew. Not everything. But enough.

“Like she’s not afraid of the ocean,” Lono said. “Like she thinks she owns it.”

Kai exhaled. “Careful. She might.”

A gust of wind carried the scent of salt and sunscreen. The crowd murmured. Sophie rode the wave to its end, executed a perfect floaters as the lip pitched forward, then stepped off clean.

She turned. Scanned the shore. Her eyes found Lono.

He didn’t look away.

She didn’t smile. But she nodded.

One nod. A silent challenge.

The ocean held its breath.

Lono stepped into the water. The cold bit his calves. He didn’t paddle out. Not yet. He waited. Watched.

Sophie paddled toward him, her board slicing through the surface like a knife.

“You ride like a man who’s already dead,” she said when she was close enough.

Lono met her stare. “Or like a man who’s got nothing left to lose.”

She held his gaze. A beat. Two.

Then she smiled. Just a little. Just enough.

“Then you should fear nothing,” she said.

She paddled past him, toward the lineup, leaving Lono standing in the surf, the water rising around his waist.

Kai snapped a photo. The flash caught Lono mid-turn, eyes on Sophie, the sky behind him like a painted scrim.

Then the next wave hit. The horizon darkened. The ocean roared.

And Lono knew—whatever game he was playing, Sophie Laurent had just become his opponent.

Chapter 2

The Sunset Beach community center smelled like coconut sunscreen and stale beer, the air thick with the hum of reporters and the shuffle of sponsor lanyards. Lono Kapule leaned against the far wall, arms crossed, watching the crowd through mirrored aviators. The Legends Tour wildcard qualifier press conference was a circus—Sophie Laurent in a butter-yellow sundress, Jax Dawson laughing too loud with a beer he hadn’t paid for, Kai Ulu lurking near the snack table with a camera around his neck like he was ready to bolt.

Governor Hokuokalani stood at the podium, adjusting his silk lapels. The microphone screeched. A cameraman cursed under his breath.

“Good afternoon,” Hokuokalani said, voice smooth as polished koa. “Today, we honor the finest surfers the North Shore has to offer.”

Lono tapped his boot against the linoleum. Something heavy sat in his pocket—a USB drive with Kai’s raw footage from the Pipeline sets. Proof. Not that he needed it. The ocean already knew.

Hokuokalani cleared his throat. “But not all legends are born in the water.”

A slide clicked onto the projector screen behind him. A blurry photo of a boy in cutoffs, grinning at Waimea Bay, waves crashing behind him. The crowd murmured. Lono’s jaw tightened.

“This,” Hokuokalani said, “is the real Lono Kapule.”

The image changed. A video played silently—Lono flipping down the face of a fifty-foot wave at Pipeline, the tube swallowing him whole, emerging like a god. The room went dead quiet.

Sophie Laurent sipped from a coconut water bottle, eyes flicking between the screen and Lono. A smirk tugged at her lips. “Well. That’s not you.”

Lono didn’t move. The USB drive dug into his palm.

Hokuokalani spread his hands. “An orphan from Kahaluu, raised by driftwood and delusions. No sponsors, no lineage. Just a man who found a camera crew willing to rewrite history.”

Jax Dawson let out a bark of laughter. “Man, that’s cold.”

The crowd tittered. Kai’s camera whirred, but his face was a mask.

Lono pushed off the wall. His boots scuffed the floor. Every eye turned. He walked to the podium, slow as a man with nothing to lose.

“Governor,” he said, voice low enough for only the front row to hear. “You ever surf Pipeline?”

Hokuokalani blinked. “Excuse me?”

Lono tossed his sunglasses onto the podium. “Let’s find out.”

The room erupted. Reporters shoved forward. Sophie set her bottle down with a quiet *clink*.

Hokuokalani’s smile didn’t reach his eyes. “You’re not qualified.”

“Neither is that photo.”

A producer tried to step between them. Lono sidestepped, scanning the crowd until he found Kai—standing frozen, camera dangling. Their eyes locked. Kai’s Adam’s apple bobbed.

Malia Hoopai pushed through the press pack, her board bag slung over one shoulder. Dark eyes locked onto Lono’s. “What the hell are you doing?”

Lono didn’t look away from Kai. “Ask him.”

Kai shoved the camera into a reporter’s hands. “I—this isn’t what—”

Hokuokalani’s voice cracked like a whip. “Enough. Security, remove him.”

Two burly men in Legends Tour polos grabbed Kai’s arms. He didn’t resist.

Sophie set a hand on Malia’s shoulder. “Let’s go.”

Malia shook it off. “Not yet.”

The projector light flickered. The video of Lono riding Pipeline still played on the screen, silent but undeniable. Hokuokalani’s face darkened.

Lono stepped closer. “You want to talk about fraud? Talk about the man who sells the North Shore to French investors.”

The room stilled. Sophie’s smile vanished.

Hokuokalani’s fingers twitched against the podium. “You know nothing.”

“Funny,” Lono said. “The ocean remembers.”

Malia’s breath hitched.

Then the doors at the back slammed open. A gust of trade wind rushed in, carrying the scent of salt and diesel. Kai’s phone buzzed in someone’s pocket—a sharp *bzzzt*—the screen glowing blue in the dim room.

A reporter gasped. “Look at this!”

Every head turned.

Kai’s face lit up the Associated Press feed on the big screen—*Journalist Kai Ulu publishes an article titled "The Prince of the Pipeline? The Secret Surfing Legacy of Lono Kapule."*

The room exploded.

Sophie’s coconut water bottle shattered on the floor.

Hokuokalani’s knuckles whitened around the podium.

Lono didn’t smile. He just turned toward the exit, boots eating up the linoleum, the weight of the USB drive suddenly lighter in his pocket.

Malia called after him, but the door swung shut behind him before he could hear.

Chapter 3

The shack smelled like old fish and salt-crusted rope. Lono crouched in the dark, fingers prying up a floorboard with the tip of his dive knife. The Kapule signet ring glinted under the single bare bulb, dull as old gold. He picked it up, rolled it in his palm. Thirty years of lies, and the metal still carried the weight of a kingdom.

A knock rattled the doorframe.

Lono didn’t move. “Come in, Kai. Or don’t. Your call.”

The door groaned open. Kai Ulu stepped inside, rain-damp board shorts clinging to his thighs, a six-pack dangling from one hand. He paused, like he expected a hug. When none came, he set the beer on the rickety table and slumped onto the milk crate Lono used for a chair.

“You saw the story,” Kai said.

Lono wiped the ring clean on his thigh, tucked it back under the floorboard. “Yep.”

The silence stretched. Kai cracked a beer, took a long pull. The can hissed. “I shouldn’t have posted it. Not like that.”

Lono swiped two cans, popped the tops with his thumbnail. “You mean not before I was ready? Or not before Sophie Laurent decided I was yesterday’s news?”

Kai flinched. “Both.”

Lono drank. The beer tasted like copper and regret. “You wanted to see me bleed. Now you get the full show.”

Kai’s eyes flicked to the floorboard. “What’s that?”

“Nothing you need to know about.”

“Lono⁠—”

“You had your shot to tell the story right. You blew it.” Lono tossed the USB drive onto the table between them. It skidded into Kai’s beer, leaving a wet trail. “There’s everything. Raw footage, interviews, the journal scans. The whole damn fairy tale.”

Kai didn’t touch it. “You’re giving this to me?”

“No. I’m giving it to the world.” Lono leaned back, boots flat on the table. “You just get to watch the fireworks first.”

Kai’s jaw worked. “I thought you needed time.”

“Time?” Lono laughed, sharp and humorless. “I don’t have time. Hokuokalani’s got investors circling like sharks at dusk. Sophie’s already whispering in Malia’s ear, planning the wedding that’ll bury my bloodline under French concrete.” He gestured at the shack. “This place? This is a postcard from the past. I’m living on borrowed time.”

Kai’s fingers tapped the USB drive. “And me?”

“You’re the fuel.” Lono stood, boots thudding on the warped floor. “You wanted to see the truth? There it is. But remember—ocean remembers. And so do I.”

Kai swallowed hard. “What happens now?”

Lono grabbed a wetsuit from the hook by the door, shook it out. “Now? I go surf.”

“Surf? After everything?”

“Especially after everything.” Lono pulled the wetsuit on over still-damp skin, zipped it slow. “The Legends Tour finale’s in a month. One clean ride at Waimea could bury Hokuokalani’s deal before it’s signed.” He shrugged into his faded Patagonia vest, no logo, no sponsors. “Or get me killed trying.”

Kai’s phone buzzed in his pocket. He fished it out, glanced at the screen, frowned. “You’re not going to Waimea tonight.”

“Why not?”

“Because—” Kai turned the phone so Lono could see the email preview: *Subject: Urgent. Meeting at Waimea Bay. Tonight.*

Lono read it twice. “Who sent it?”

Kai shook his head. “No signature. Just that.”

Lono looked at the ceiling, as if the answer might drip from the rafters. “Sophie’s got eyes everywhere. Hokuokalani’s got spies on every beach. Someone’s testing the water.”

Kai stood abruptly, chair screeching. “You can’t go. Not now. Not like this.”

Lono tossed him the keys to the rusted ’78 Bronco parked crooked in the sand. “Lock up for me. And Kai?”

Kai caught the keys, one-handed.

“Burn the footage. All of it.” Lono adjusted his gloves, fingers tapping the signet ring through the wetsuit fabric. “Or don’t. I don’t care anymore.”

He stepped out into the rain, salt and wet wind stinging his face. The Bronco’s engine coughed to life behind him. Kai didn’t offer a ride.

Lono walked toward the beach, boots sinking in the sand. The North Shore’s winter swell growled in the distance, a beast waking from a long sleep. He could hear it from here—Waimea Bay, grinding its teeth against the reef. The perfect place to die or be reborn.

His phone buzzed in his vest pocket. He pulled it out, thumb hovering over the screen. Not Kai. Not Sophie. A blocked number.

He almost answered.

Almost.

Then he flipped the phone shut, dropped it back in his pocket. The Bronco’s headlights cut through the dark as Kai peeled out, tires spitting sand.

Lono kept walking.

Behind him, the laptop on the shack’s desk glowed, the email still unread—the subject line pulsing like a heartbeat in the dark.

Chapter 4

The Turtle Bay Resort’s grand lawn spilled toward the dark Pacific, torches flickering like captured stars. Hokuokalani stood beneath a banyan tree draped in fairy lights, a glass of amber whiskey in hand. The decanter, cut with the Kapule family crest, rested on the railing behind him. He swirled the liquid once, caught the light, then drank.

Lono watched from the edge of the crowd, sunglasses pushed up into his hair, a cracked coconut in his grip. The juice ran down his wrist, sticky and warm.

“You’re late,” Hokuokalani’s daughter said beside him.

Malia Hoopai wore a halter dress the color of sunset over Waimea, heels sinking into the grass. Lono didn’t look at her. “So are you.”

She exhaled through her nose, a sound that might’ve been a laugh. “Duty calls. Unlike some people.”

Lono licked salt from his lips. “Duty’s a funny word. Sounds like something you inherit.”

Malia’s fingers tightened around her glass. A gold bangle slid down her wrist, catching the light. “You’ve got nerve, Kapule.”

The name hung between them, sharp enough to cut. Lono didn’t flinch. “I’m not the one borrowing titles.”

A server passed with shrimp skewers. Malia took two, held one out to Lono. He declined with a shake of his head.

Sophie Laurent glided toward them, barefoot on the grass, her red dress clinging where the ocean mist kissed it. “Malia, darling,” she purred, sliding an arm around the younger woman’s waist. “You look like you’re about to be auctioned.”

Malia didn’t pull away.

Lono tilted the coconut to his mouth, draining the last of it. The ice had melted hours ago.

Sophie’s eyes flicked to him, sharp as fins cutting water. “Lono. Still pretending to be human, I see.”

“Sophie.” He wiped his mouth with the back of his hand. “Still pretending to be French?”

Her smile didn’t waver. “Only when necessary.”

Hokuokalani raised his glass, the crystal decanter glinting in the torchlight. “Friends! Investors! Patriots of progress!” The crowd hushed, forks pausing mid-air. “Tonight, we toast the end of fraud. The end of impostors. The dawn of a new era—one where true Hawaiians reap the rewards of their own shores.”

Glasses clinked. Malia’s rang hollow against Sophie’s, the sound brittle.

Lono tossed the coconut into a trash bin. It bounced off the rim, rolled into the sand.

A hand clamped his shoulder. Kai, in a borrowed button-down, breath smelling of beer and bad decisions. “You’re supposed to be hiding,” he muttered under his breath.

Lono shrugged free. “Not my style.”

Kai’s fingers twitched, like he wanted to drag Lono into the bushes. Instead, he leaned in, voice low. “The decanter. It’s—”

“Kapule,” Lono finished.

Kai’s jaw tightened. “You let him drink from your family’s crystal?”

Lono watched the governor swallow another mouthful. “He doesn’t know. And he won’t. Not until it’s too late.”

Malia moved closer, close enough that the scent of gardenias and saltwater wrapped around Lono’s ribs. “You’re playing a dangerous game, surfer boy.”

He turned his head. She was inches away, her gaze locked onto his mouth. “I don’t play games.”

Sophie’s laugh cut through the moment, bright and false. “Oh, but you do. Just not the ones people expect.”

Malia’s hand found Lono’s wrist, her thumb pressing into the pulse point. “Leave him alone, Sophie. He’s not worth the dirt under your nails.”

Sophie’s smile vanished. “Careful, Malia. Loyalty to a fraud won’t buy you a throne.”

Malia’s nails bit into Lono’s skin. “I didn’t ask for a throne. But I know a fake when I see one.”

Lono pulled his arm back. The exchange left a silence that tasted like gunpowder.

The governor cleared his throat, glass raised again. “To progress! To the future!”

The crowd echoed, voices rising like a rogue set building. Malia drained her drink in one swallow. Sophie’s fingers drummed against her thigh, nails painted the color of dried blood.

Kai muttered, “We need to go.”

Lono didn’t move. “Not yet.”

Hokuokalani’s phone buzzed in his breast pocket. He pulled it out, glanced at the screen, and paled. The glass slipped from his fingers, shattering on the wooden deck. Whiskey pooled like dark blood.

Sophie’s voice dropped to a whisper. “What is it?”

The governor didn’t answer. He stared at the phone as if it were a live grenade.

Kai’s phone buzzed in his pocket. He fished it out, frowned at the unknown number.

Malia’s gaze darted between them. “What’s happening?”

No one answered.

Hokuokalani’s thumb hovered over the screen. Then he answered.

Silence stretched. The crash of the ocean filled the void, waves eating the shore inch by inch.

Hokuokalani’s voice was a rasp. “Yes?”

A pause. The governor’s knuckles turned white.

Then, from the other end of the line—a voice, soft and clear:

“He’s not gone. He’s coming.”

Chapter 5

The North Shore swallowed ambition whole.

Hokuokalani stood knee-deep in the churn at Pipeline, board tucked under his arm like a shield. The water clawed at his calves, cold and relentless. Around him, surfers watched from the lineup, arms crossed, faces unreadable. He didn’t look at them. Couldn’t. Not with the cameras on him—one mounted on a jet ski, another on a drone humming overhead.

"You sure about this?" Jax called from the shore. The old-timer’s voice carried over the wind, rough as coral. "That reef don’t forgive."

Hokuokalani adjusted his leash, fingers slipping on the wax. "I’m not asking for permission."

Jax spat into the water. "Then don’t cry when it takes your leg instead."

The governor ignored him. He waded deeper, suit clinging to his thighs. The set was building—three waves already sucking up the channel, dark walls rising like cathedral spires. He waited for the fourth. The one he could ride. The one that would prove he wasn’t just a man with a title and a grudge.

The wave came.

Hokuokalani paddled hard, arms burning. The water turned to glass beneath him, then shattered. He popped up, tucked the board under his stomach, and kicked. The wave swallowed him whole.

For three seconds, it was perfect.

Then the lip pitched forward, sharp as a guillotine.

Hokuokalani tucked into a crouch, board teetering beneath him. The wave jacked him up, higher, higher—until he was the tallest thing on the water, a king on a throne of whitewater. He grinned. This was what they didn’t understand. Power wasn’t asking. It was taking.

The bottom dropped out.

The wave folded. Hokuokalani pitched forward, board flying from his feet. He hit the water feet-first, the impact knocking the breath from his lungs. The reef rose up to meet him.

Pain exploded in his calf.

Hokuokalani screamed into the saltwater, a sound swallowed by the ocean. His board, now a wild thing, whipped back toward him. The leash snapped. The board careened into his ribs, driving him deeper.

Blood spiraled into the blue.

He kicked, frantic, but the current had him. The reef was a razor’s edge beneath his skin. He grabbed for the board, missed, grabbed again. His fingers closed around the rail just as the next wave hit.

The surfers on the lineup exchanged glances.

"That’s not how it works," one muttered.

Hokuokalani dragged himself onto his board, blood clouding the water around him. His calf throbbed, hot and wet. He pressed a hand to the wound, came away with red. His vision swam.

The jet ski roared toward him. A cameraman in a black wetsuit leaned over the edge, helmet cam glowing red.

"Governor!" the man shouted over the wind. "You alright?"

Hokuokalani’s teeth were clenched. "I’m fine."

The cameraman didn’t look convinced. He turned the jet ski, filming as Hokuokalani paddled toward shore.

The crowd on the beach was a blur. Hokuokalani focused on the sand, on putting one hand in front of the other. He didn’t look up until he collapsed onto the shore, gasping.

A lifeguard was there in seconds, kneeling beside him. "Sir, we need to get you to the hospital."

Hokuokalani waved him off. "No. I’m fine."

But he wasn’t.

The drone hovered above, its lens capturing everything—Hokuokalani’s blood in the water, the fear in his eyes, the way his hands shook as he pressed them to his leg.

Sophie Laurent stepped forward, her red dress fluttering in the wind. "Hoku," she said, voice sharp. "What happened?"

He didn’t answer. Couldn’t. The taste of salt and iron filled his mouth.

Then the feed cut to the big screen at Banzai Pipeline.

The wipeout played in slow motion. Hokuokalani’s face, twisted in pain. The blood, spreading like ink in water. The moment he realized he wasn’t the predator here.

The crowd murmured. Some laughed.

Malia Hoopai stood at the edge of the scene, her surfboard tucked under her arm. Her knuckles were white. She didn’t look at her father.

Lono Kapule was there too, leaning against a lifeguard tower, arms crossed. He didn’t smile. Didn’t gloat. Just watched, the way a man watches a storm—waiting to see if it would break.

Hokuokalani’s phone buzzed in his pocket. He fished it out, nearly dropping it. The screen glowed with notifications—messages, calls, missed ones. All from Sophie. All from investors. All demanding answers.

He ignored them. Looked up at the screen.

His wipeout was trending.

#3 on the North Shore cams.

#1 on the Legends Tour feed.

The world was watching.

And for the first time, Hokuokalani didn’t like what they saw.

Chapter 6

The newsroom smelled like stale coffee and printer ink. Kai Ulu sat in the back corner, fingers hovering over the keyboard, the glow of his monitor casting long shadows on the cubicle walls. Behind him, half a dozen reporters hunched over desks, phones pressed to ears, voices rising and falling in urgent bursts. A TV mounted in the corner played the latest surf cam footage—Hokuokalani’s wipeout at Pipeline, slowed to a crawl, the reef a jagged grin beneath him.

Kai exhaled through his nose and opened a new document.

*No signature. No trail.*

A hand clapped his shoulder. "You pulling another all-nighter, kid?"

Kai didn’t look up. "Just checking sources."

Dale, the city editor, leaned against the cubicle. "Sources, huh? Since when do you have sources?"

"Since I started covering the right people."

Dale chuckled, but there was no humor in it. "You’re gonna get yourself fired. Or worse."

Kai’s fingers didn’t stop moving. "Not my first time."

He attached a file to an email, no subject line, no body. Just a zip archive labeled *North Shore—06*. He hovered the cursor over Send, then minimized the window. His phone buzzed in his pocket. A text from an unknown number: *You sure about this?*

Kai texted back: *No. But I’m sure about him.*

He hit Send.

The archive vanished into the void of the internet, leaving no footprint, no receipt. Kai closed his eyes. For three years, he’d been Lono’s shadow. Three years of half-truths and coded signals, of surf cams and backroom deals and the constant, gnawing fear that one wrong move would unravel everything.

This was the wrong move.

His desk phone rang. Kai ignored it. The newsroom hummed around him, a living thing, hungry and unrelenting. He pulled up a browser, typed in a local station’s tip line, and uploaded the satellite images he’d stitched together over the past month.

Red zones. Yellow zones. Dumping pipes spewing dark plumes into the turquoise water.

The article practically wrote itself.

---

Waimea Bay at dawn was a graveyard of foam and memory. The swell was smaller here, gentler, the kind of wave that let surfers breathe. Lono stood on the sand, board tucked under his arm, the wind biting through his rash guard. He’d been watching the news all morning. The North Shore was in an uproar—Hokuokalani’s wipeout, the leaked waste dumping scandal, Sophie Laurent’s sudden appearance as the face of the Legends Tour.

Something was coming.

He didn’t see Kai until the kid stepped out from behind a lifeguard tower, hands in his pockets, face drawn.

"You did it," Lono said.

Kai didn’t deny it. "I had to."

"At what cost?"

"Mine."

Lono studied him. Kai looked like he’d aged ten years overnight. Dark circles under his eyes, stubble he hadn’t bothered to shave, the kind of tension in his shoulders that came from too many sleepless nights and too much caffeine.

"You could’ve told me."

"I couldn’t. Not until it was done."

Lono turned toward the water. The swell was building, the horizon thickening with dark, rolling peaks. "Hokuokalani’s gonna come for you."

"Let him."

"Kai—"

"I know what I’m doing."

Lono wanted to argue. Wanted to grab him by the collar and shake him until he saw sense. But the kid was already walking away, his sneakers kicking up sand.

The lifeguard tower creaked as Lono leaned against it, his board digging into the wood. He pulled out his phone. One missed call from an unknown number. No voicemail. Typical.

A shadow fell across the sand.

Malia Hoopai stood there, arms crossed, her board under one arm. She wore a faded Quiksilver tee, the sleeves rolled up to her elbows, her dark hair pulled into a messy braid. No makeup. No pretense.

"You’re not surprised," she said.

Lono pocketed his phone. "About the dumping? Or the fact that your father’s an idiot?"

Malia smirked. "Both."

She dropped her board into the sand and sat beside it, knees drawn up. Lono followed suit, their shoulders nearly touching.

"You hate him," Lono said.

Malia didn’t answer right away. Watched the surfers in the lineup, the way they paddled out, waited, poised. "I hate what he’s become."

"Same thing."

She glanced at him, quick and sharp. "You ever think about what *you’re* becoming?"

Lono flexed his fingers, feeling the calluses on his palms. "Every day."

Malia laughed, low and bitter. "Funny. You look like you’ve got it all figured out."

Lono didn’t laugh. "I don’t."

Silence settled between them, comfortable in its way. The ocean roared in the distance, the sound of a wave breaking over the reef. Malia tilted her head. "You ever surf Waimea at night?"

Lono raised an eyebrow. "Once. Didn’t end well."

"Tonight’s your lucky night."

"Why?"

She stood, brushing sand off her thighs. "Because the qualifier’s postponed. Indefinitely." She nodded toward the beach, where a crowd was gathering, voices rising in frustration. "And because my father’s about to lose everything."

Lono watched her walk away, her board under her arm, the morning light catching the gold in her hair. The crowd’s murmurs grew louder. Then, cutting through the noise, a single voice—loud, angry, desperate:

"Kapule!"

The word hung in the air like a curse.

Lono’s breath caught.

Malia froze.

Heads turned. Shoulders stiffened. A cameraman on the beach swung his lens toward the sound.

Lono didn’t run. Didn’t hide. Just stood there, board in hand, the name echoing in his chest like a second heartbeat.

The crowd surged forward, phones raised, eyes wide. The qualifier was supposed to be the first step toward the Legends Tour finale. Now it was over. Now everything was in the open.

And Lono Kapule was the only one who knew what came next.

He looked at the water. The swell was perfect. The reef was waiting.

He stepped onto his board.

Chapter 7

The lineup at Pipeline was empty except for Sophie Laurent, her white board cutting a sharp line against the deep blue of the reef. The judges’ tower loomed behind her, a metal skeleton against the gray morning sky. A hundred spectators huddled on the beach, phones raised, waiting for her to fail.

Sophie cleared her throat. The mic in her hand crackled.

“I want to thank everyone for coming,” she began, voice steady at first. Then it wobbled—just a flicker, a hesitation. She gripped the mic tighter. “The spirit of aloha… is about respect. About honor. About knowing your place in the ocean and in the world.”

A murmur rippled through the crowd. Someone laughed.

Sophie’s jaw tightened. She raised her voice above the noise. “We are guardians of these waves. Not owners. Not developers. Not—”

A sharp whistle cut her off. A local in a faded NO BROWN WATER shirt cupped his hands around his mouth. “Then why’d you bring your daddy’s bulldozers to Sunset?”

Sophie faltered. The mic picked up the wet slap of her palm turning slick. She didn’t look at the crowd. Didn’t look at the judges. Her gaze locked onto Malia Hoopai, standing twenty feet away, arms crossed, smirking like she’d just won a bet.

“You’re not him,” Malia said, loud enough for half the beach to hear.

Sophie’s breath hitched. She blinked once, twice. Then her back stiffened. She turned away from Malia and forced a smile. “Let’s focus on surfing today. The Legends Tour qualifier is about skill, not speculation.”

A cameraman on the beach zoomed in. Sophie’s face filled the screen—lips pressed thin, eyes burning with something between rage and fear.

Malia stepped closer, boots sinking into the damp sand. “You ever wonder why he never showed up again? The real prince, I mean.”

Sophie’s fingers twitched around the mic. “I don’t care about fairy tales.”

“Funny. Neither did I. Until today.” Malia tilted her head toward the water. “Looks like the waves don’t care about fairy tales either.”

Sophie followed her gaze. A set rolled in—three perfect peaks, glassy and hungry. The kind of wave that made legends. The kind that swallowed outsiders whole.

Sophie’s white board drifted toward the water on its own, as if pulled by a tide only she could feel. She didn’t retrieve it.

Malia laughed. “Guess you’re up. Unless you’d rather hide behind a speech.”

Sophie’s phone buzzed in her pocket. She ignored it. The judges were waving her out. The crowd was chanting. Her face was on every screen from Haleiwa to Waikiki.

She waded into the shallows, board under her arm, saltwater already soaking through her leggings. The first wave of the set reared up behind her, a blue wall topped with white foam. She didn’t look back.

The buzzer sounded.

The wave broke.

Sophie paddled hard, heart hammering. Halfway to the lineup, her phone buzzed again. She glanced down.

A text lit up the screen, unfamiliar number:

*He’s at the Kapule Estate. Now.*

Her paddle slipped. Salt stung her eyes. She blinked it away and focused on the wave—the curl, the power, the moment before it swallowed you.

She ducked under.

The world went dark.

When she surfaced, gasping, the wave was gone. The lineup was empty. Her board floated upside down, leash stretched taut.

A shadow fell over her.

Malia stood on the beach, phone in hand, filming. “You gonna bail before you even start?”

Sophie wiped saltwater from her lips. “I don’t need a ride.”

Malia dropped the phone into her pocket and offered a hand.

Sophie hesitated. Then she took it.

Chapter 8

The surf shop in Haleiwa smelled like resin and stale beer at 6 a.m. A yellow police line fluttered in the parking lot where broken glass glittered under a streetlamp. Lono wiped salt from his temple and tossed a burlap sack onto the counter.

"I need a new leash," he said.

Moke, the owner, didn't look up from his coffee. "You're lucky you still got feet."

Lono peeled a twenty off his wad. "Add a bar of reef wax. Mostly white."

Moke took the cash without counting. Slid the leash across the counter. "They're saying it was dynamite. Someone went full kamikaze on Hoku's gear."

Lono pressed the wax to his palm. "Must've been a real kamikaze."

Moke sniffed, poured himself another cup. "You hear about Sophie?"

Lono paused. "She quit mid-speech."

"Didn't just quit. She paddled out. Alone." Moke's eyes flicked to Lono's. "Onset line at Pipeline. Didn't catch a single wave."

Lono finished wrapping the leash, tested the knot. "She's got a sponsor."

"Yeah, and sponsors don't surf Pipeline in January unless they're made of steel." Moke leaned in, voice low. "They're calling it sabotage. Hoku's boys are on edge."

Lono walked out, board under arm. The street was empty except for a rental truck idling at the stop sign. Two men in sunglasses watched him from inside. He didn't look back.

---

The Kapule Estate sat behind iron gates, bougainvillea curling over the rusted tops. Lono parked the Jeep on the old carriage path where the sand met the pavement. The estate itself was a skeleton—peeling paint, boarded windows, the grand staircase leading nowhere. But the reef out front still carried the force of thirty years of swells, the deep blue channel marking the royal breaks.

His footprints were the only ones in the fresh sand.

He unlocked the side door, stepped into the cool dim of the old study. The air smelled of mildew and old paper. The signet ring was still where he'd left it—wrapped in cloth in the bottom drawer of the mahogany desk. He slipped it into his pocket. The journal was gone.

His fingers paused over the empty space.

A floorboard creaked upstairs.

Lono went still. The back door was still locked. No windows broken. No forced entry. Just footprints leading to the door. His.

He took the stairs slow, the wood groaning under his weight. The second floor was gutted—rooms stripped to studs, wiring hanging like dead nerves. The only furniture left was a single chair in the master bedroom, facing the ocean. A man sat in it, back to the door.

Governor Hokuokalani didn't turn around. "You always were a terrible liar."

Lono stayed in the doorway. "I don't remember inviting you in."

Hokuokalani's fingers tapped the armrest. A gold band glinted on his pinky. "Your security chief thinks you did this." He gestured vaguely at the empty room. "Sabotage. To scare me off. To look like a hero when the deal collapses."

Lono stepped forward. "I didn't touch your bulldozers."

"You didn’t have to." Hokuokalani finally turned, his face shadowed. "The market did. Investors see blood in the water. They smell panic. The privatization deal is dead."

Lono felt the ring heavy in his pocket. "Guess you'll have to find another way to steal the shore."

Hokuokalani's smile was a knife slash. "There are other ways. But today? Today, we celebrate." He reached into his jacket, pulled out a folded page. "Front page of the Star-Advertiser. Sophie Laurent quit the tour in her bathing suit. Malia Hoopai walked out with Lono Kapule's biggest rival. And you?" He unfolded the paper. A photo of Lono barreling at Pipeline, arms out, eyes closed in rapture. The headline read: *The Prince of Pipeline.*

Hokuokalani tapped the photo. "They're calling you a legend. Like you were born from the sea."

Lono didn't take the paper. "What do you want?"

"What I’ve always wanted. Obedience." Hokuokalani folded the paper back. "Ride for us, Lono. One more season. Let the board vote. Let the crowds roar. And in return?" He leaned forward. "Your past stays buried."

Lono stared at the ocean through the broken window. The set was building offshore—hollow thunder. A perfect wave forming.

He said, "I don't take orders from coup kings."

Hokuokalani's jaw tightened. His phone buzzed in his pocket. He pulled it out, glanced at the screen. His face went slack.

Lono saw the name light up the screen before Hokuokalani shoved it away. Malia.

The governor stood abruptly, knocking the chair back. "One week, Lono. Then the board meets without you." He strode past, shoulder brushing Lono's. "Stay away from my daughter."

Lono didn't move until he heard the car engine roar to life. Then he pulled out his own phone. One missed call. Malia's number.

He played the voicemail.

Silence. Then Malia's voice, low and steady. "Dad. I'm with him."

Lono's thumb hovered over the delete button. The wave outside crashed against the reef, a deep, resonant groan. He didn't delete it.

Chapter 9

Waimea Bay was a battlefield of wind and water. The February swell clawed at the shore, sending up plumes of white mist that stung Lono’s face as he carried his board across the black sand. The food truck’s red awning flapped like a warning flag. Hokuokalani stood in its shadow, hands in his pockets, watching the lineup.

Lono didn’t slow down.

“You’re late,” Hokuokalani said.

“You’re blocking the order,” Lono replied.

A line of locals snaked past them, hands clutching greasy paper, steam curling into the damp air. Hokuokalani didn’t step aside. He just tilted his head toward the truck. “Two pupus. One shave ice. Extra syrup.”

Lono dropped his board in the sand. “I’m not here to eat.”

“No. You’re here to starve.” Hokuokalani’s fingers drummed the menu board. “Funny. The best waves don’t care how hungry you are.”

Lono stared at the chalk-dusted menu. The words blurred. “What do you want?”

Hokuokalani leaned against the truck’s side panel. “Same thing I’ve always wanted. Control.”

A gust of wind tore a napkin from someone’s hand and sent it skittering into the ocean. Lono watched it dissolve into the froth.

Hokuokalani’s voice dropped. “Ride for us, Lono. One last season. You know the board votes in March. Let them crown you. Let them cheer. Let them think you’re one of them.” He tapped the menu where a faded photo of a shave ice mountain stared back. “And in return, your secrets stay buried.”

Lono turned his head. His eyes caught the horizon where the first real set of the day reared up like a dark beast. A boarder beneath it arched, disappeared into the barrel.

Hokuokalani followed his gaze. “That could be you tomorrow. Bigger than Pipeline. Bigger than Sophie Laurent. The people will love you. The sponsors will beg. You’ll have the world eating out of your hand.”

Lono exhaled through his nose. “I don’t take orders from coup kings.”

Hokuokalani didn’t flinch. “Then take a gift.” He reached into his shirt pocket, pulled out a folded slip of paper. “Front row seats to the Legends Tour finale. A golden ticket. A lifetime pass.” He unfolded it, held it out. The paper crackled in the wind. “Sign it, and I’ll make sure your father’s name is cleared.”

Lono didn’t touch it. “That’s not how this works.”

“How does it work, then?” Hokuokalani’s voice sharpened. “You wave your hands in the air and hope the crowd mistakes luck for destiny? You ride one impossible wave and suddenly you’re the chosen one?” He crumpled the paper in his fist. “The world’s already decided. You’re either with us or you’re against us.”

A laugh cut through the air. Malia Hoopai stood at the edge of the crowd, her board tucked under her arm, her hair tied back with a neon yellow cord. She wasn’t looking at them. She was watching the water.

Lono felt the ring in his pocket like a stone.

Hokuokalani didn’t notice her. “One week, Lono. Then the board meets without you. Stay away from my daughter. Stay away from Sophie. Stay away from the past.” He shoved the crumpled paper into Lono’s chest. “Or I’ll drag it all into the light myself.”

The wind howled. The wave roared. Malia dropped her board and paddled out, catching the next set with effortless grace.

Lono let the paper fall into the sand.

Hokuokalani’s phone buzzed in his jacket. He fished it out, glanced at the screen. His eyebrows twitched.

Lono saw the name before the governor could hide it.

Malia.

Hokuokalani’s thumb hovered over the screen. The line on his forehead deepened. Then he shoved the phone away without answering.

Lono watched him walk back to his car, the crumpled paper still lying in the sand like a rejected omen.

The shave ice vendor wiped his hands on his apron and sighed. “You want the damn ice or not?”

Lono looked at the ocean. Malia was already in the lineup, waiting for the next set. Her stroke was fluid, controlled. She wasn’t looking back.

The phone buzzed again. This time, Lono heard the faint chime from Hokuokalani’s pocket. The governor froze for half a second before yanking it out.

He stared at the screen.

Then he stepped into the wind, his voice cracking over the noise of the crowd.

“Dad. I’m with him.”

Chapter 10

The back room of Kai’s condo smelled like salt and old coffee. Microchips lined the workbench like broken seashells. Lono leaned against the wall, arms crossed, watching the screen flicker with green lines of code. Sophie stood by the window, arms wrapped around herself, her surfboard propped in the corner like a silent accusation.

Kai’s fingers danced over the keyboard. “You sure you want to do this?”

“You hacked a governor’s server on my say-so last week,” Lono said. “You worried now?”

“I’m worried your life just got smaller.” Kai didn’t look up. “Not everyone wants to know what’s buried.”

Sophie shifted. “I want to know.”

Kai hit enter. The screen flashed white. A folder appeared. “There. Private contracts. Public beaches. Signatures.”

Lono stepped closer. The glow from the monitor painted his face in sharp relief. The first document was titled *North Shore Privatization Initiative*.

Hokuokalani’s signature was bold at the bottom.

Sophie leaned in. “That’s—”

“Real,” Kai said. “And here.” He clicked again. A second file opened, smaller, titled *1975 Transition Records*. Lono’s stomach tightened.

Sophie’s breath hitched. “That’s not public.”

Kai scrolled. Footnotes referenced land transfers. Names. Dates. And at the bottom, in italics: *Family Business: Hokuokalani (deceased), involvement confirmed.*

Lono’s pulse hammered in his throat. “Deceased?”

Kai flipped to the next page. A grainy photo showed a man in a suit shaking hands with a uniformed officer. The caption read *Captain Hokuokalani, assisting in the orderly transition.*

Lono’s father’s face wasn’t in the frame, but Lono knew.

Sophie exhaled. “That’s why they wanted to marry me to your brother. They needed the merger to hide the land deals.”

The room went still. Kai’s chair creaked as he swiveled toward them. “Your brother?”

Sophie’s eyes flicked to Lono. “Jax. He was supposed to marry me. Seal the deal. Make the privatization look like a family legacy.”

Lono stared at the screen. The truth sat there, cold and undeniable. Hokuokalani hadn’t just stolen land. He’d bought silence.

Kai’s phone buzzed. He glanced at it, frowning. “Hokuokalani’s server just lit up like a Christmas tree. He’s in it.”

Sophie exhaled sharply. “He knows.”

Lono grabbed his board. “We go now.”

Kai held up a hand. “Wait—there’s more.”

He clicked. A third file. Lono’s intestines twisted.

*Kapule Dynasty: Rightful Heir – Lono Kapule.* A birth certificate. A coronation draft. And at the very bottom, a single line: *Signet ring hidden in estate.*

Sophie gasped. “That’s impossible.”

Lono didn’t move. He felt the ring in his pocket, warm against his thigh.

Kai’s eyes flicked between them. “You knew?”

Lono didn’t answer. Sophie’s face went pale. “You’re—”

The window rattled. Wind howled outside, rattling the slats. A gust blew through the room, sending papers skittering. Kai cursed and reached for them.

Sophie stepped between them. “You’re the lost prince.”

Lono looked at her. “I’m a surfer.”

Kai’s computer chimed—a remote alert. He glanced at the screen. “He’s sending cleanup.”

Sophie’s eyes widened. “He’ll kill us.”

Lono moved. He grabbed his board and yanked the door open. The hallway outside was empty, but the stairwell echoed with footsteps. Kai stuffed papers into a bag, hands shaking.

Sophie followed Lono out, her own board under her arm. “Where do we go?”

Lono didn’t slow down. “The estate.”

Kai stumbled after them. “Your estate? That place is a tomb.”

“Exactly,” Lono said. “He won’t look there.”

They hit the parking lot. The wind had picked up, flinging sand across the asphalt like shrapnel. Kai’s car was a hundred yards away, parked under a flickering streetlight. Lono tossed his board into the back of Kai’s jeep.

Sophie didn’t hesitate. She tossed her board in beside it and climbed in the front.

Kai jammed the keys in the ignition. “You sure about this?”

Lono didn’t answer. He pulled the ring from his pocket. The gold caught the dim light, etched with waves and a crown.

Kai’s breath caught.

Sophie turned her head, eyes wide.

Lono slipped the ring back into his pocket. “Drive.”

Chapter 11

The North Shore was a powder keg smoldering under floodlights. Waimea Bay’s whitewater threw spray into the air like a rabid crowd spewing salt. Lono stood on the beach with Kai, the jeep idling behind them, exhaust curling into the damp evening. Sophie’s rental car was parked fifty feet away. She hadn’t spoken since they left Kai’s condo.

Kai checked his watch. “Press conference starts in twenty minutes.”

Lono wiped sand from his board. “She’s already live.”

Sophie’s voice carried from the car’s open window, sharp and clear. “I’m not marrying your brother.”

The crowd around the pop-up stage gasped. Phones rose like a forest of steel trees. Sophie stood in a crisp blazer, her surfboard propped beside her, the North Shore lights glinting off her dark hair. Behind her, a giant screen displayed her face in high-definition, her words scrolling as text across the bottom of every phone screen within fifty yards.

Kai’s phone buzzed. He pulled it out, read the notification. “Malia’s streaming too.”

Lono didn’t look at it. His focus stayed on Sophie, the way the wind tugged at her jacket. “What’s Malia doing?”

“Something stupid.” Kai tapped the screen. A live feed popped up: Malia Hoopai on her balcony in Kahaluu, the Kapule Estate looming behind her in silhouette. She held a surfboard under one arm, the house lights casting long shadows.

Malia’s voice carried through the crowd’s murmurs. “I don’t want his legacy. I don’t want his deals. I don’t want his name.”

Sophie’s voice overlapped hers from Waimea. “And I don’t want to be part of a family that buys oceans.”

Kai’s screen flickered. Another notification. He cursed. “Shit. Kai Ulu just posted something.”

Lono didn’t need to see it. He felt the shift in the air, the sudden tension radiating from the crowd. Kai’s thumbs flew over the keyboard. “He says—”*Kai read aloud*—*‘The Coup in the Coral: How Hokuokalani’s Family Stole a Kingdom.’*”

Lono’s jaw tightened. “He went too far.”

Sophie’s stream cut to black. The crowd erupted. Phones buzzed in every hand. Kai’s phone lit up with replies, screens flashing like emergency beacons.

Sophie stepped off the stage, her board under her arm. She walked straight to Lono. “You knew about this?”

Lono didn’t answer. The wind howled between them.

Sophie’s eyes locked onto his. “You let him print it?”

Kai pocketed his phone. “It’s done, Soph. We handle it now.”

Sophie’s mouth twisted. “Handle it? Your so-called best friend just set the whole island on fire, Jax.”

Lono felt the name like a punch. “I’m not Jax.”

Sophie’s gaze flicked down to his chest. “No. You’re worse.”

A man in a suit pushed through the crowd toward them. Hokuokalani’s aide. His tie flapped in the wind, his face pale under the floodlights.

He stopped in front of Lono. His voice was low, urgent. “The signet ring—he’s using the ring.”

Lono’s hand twitched toward his pocket. The aide’s eyes flicked to the movement.

Sophie stepped between them. “What ring?”

The aide didn’t look at her. His voice dropped lower. “The Kapule signet. The tattoo on his chest. It’s proof.”

Kai’s breath caught. “What?”

The aide’s eyes darted between them, then away. “Hokuokalani’s been searching for it for years. If he’s using it—”

Lono grabbed the aide’s collar. Sand crunched under their shoes. “Where is he?”

The aide swallowed. “Pipeline. The Legends Tour finale starts in an hour. He’s expecting you.”

Sophie’s eyes widened. “Jax—”

Lono shoved the aide back. He turned to Kai. “Get the jeep.”

Kai didn’t move. “Lono, wait—”

Lono was already walking toward the water, his board tucked under his arm. The tide pulled at his ankles, cold and insistent.

Sophie followed. “You can’t just walk into this.”

Lono didn’t look back. “Watch me.”

Kai jogged after them. “We need a plan.”

Sophie grabbed Lono’s arm. He stopped. The water lapped at their calves, dark and hungry.

Sophie’s voice was barely audible over the wind. “If Hokuokalani knows about the ring, he knows about everything.”

Lono pulled his arm free. “Then it’s time.”

Sophie’s eyes burned into his. “Time for what?”

Lono looked at the horizon. The first stars blinked into existence over the ocean.

“Time to end it.”

Chapter 12

The pipeline at Ehukai Beach roared like a freight train gone mad. Twenty-foot walls of water pitched forward, curling into glass tubes that swallowed the light. The Legends Tour finale had drawn every lens and every bet on the North Shore. The VIP section was a sea of sponsor logos and whispers.

Lono stood on the sand, board tucked under his arm, the weight of the signet tattoo pressing against his chest. Kai’s fingers flew over a borrowed tablet. “You sure about this?”

Lono didn’t answer. His eyes tracked the ocean, the way the water sucked back before each monstrous wave. Sophie adjusted her wetsuit sleeves, her jaw set. “You’re not backing out now.”

Hokuokalani stood near the judges’ tower, his suit jacket flapping despite the evening calm. His aide hovered, whispering into his ear. When he noticed Lono, his face darkened like a storm rolling in.

Kai lowered the tablet. “He’s watching you like a target.”

Lono stepped forward, bare feet sinking into the wet sand. Sophie matched his pace. “You’re going to do this on the beach?”

Lono didn’t answer. He kept walking toward the waterline where the Legends Tour banner snapped in the wind. The crowd’s murmurs rose as he passed, a ripple moving through the bodies like wind through grass.

A reporter shoved a microphone in his face. “Lono Kapule, rumors say you’re more than just a surfer. Care to comment?”

Lono kept walking. Sophie stepped between them, her hand on the mic. “No comment.”

The reporter didn’t move. “Is it true you’re related to the Kapule Dynasty?”

Lono froze. The crowd’s noise dimmed to a low hum. Sophie’s grip on his arm tightened.

Hokuokalani’s voice cut through the tension. “Let the man speak.”

Lono turned. Hokuokalani stood ten feet away, his shadow stretching long over the sand. The governor’s eyes locked onto Lono’s chest.

Lono exhaled. Then he pulled his jersey over his head.

The crowd gasped. Phones rose like a flock of startled birds. The Kapule signet tattoo glistened under the floodlights—a coiled wave cradling a royal crest. The crowd’s murmurs exploded into a chant:

*Ka-moana! Ka-moana!*

Sophie’s breath hitched. Kai’s fingers clenched the tablet.

Hokuokalani’s face drained of color. His aide’s mouth opened and closed like a landed fish.

Lono didn’t look away. “I’m the last heir of the Kapule line.”

Hokuokalani’s voice was a blade. “You’re a fraud.”

Lono stepped closer. The sand shifted under his feet. “Prove it.”

The crowd’s chant grew louder. The ocean thundered behind them. Hokuokalani’s hands curled into fists.

A voice cut through the noise. “Prove it for me.”

Malia Hoopai stepped out from the shadows of the judges’ tower. Her surfboard hung at her side, her wetsuit clinging to her like a second skin. She held a ring between her fingers—the same crest as Lono’s tattoo, glinting under the lights.

Hokuokalani’s breath came fast. “Malia—”

She didn’t look at him. Her eyes locked onto Lono’s. “I choose the ocean.”

She slipped the ring onto her own finger. The crowd erupted. Sophie’s mouth fell open. Kai’s tablet slipped from his hands, thudding into the sand.

Lono’s chest tightened. “Malia—”

She shook her head. “Not his name. Not his deals. Not his legacy.”

Hokuokalani’s voice cracked. “You ungrateful—”

Malia turned her back on him. The ring on her finger caught the light like a defiant beacon.

The crowd’s chant shifted, louder now, more urgent:

*Ka-moana! Ka-moana!*

Lono watched Hokuokalani, the way his face twisted between fury and something worse—realization.

The governor’s voice was raw. “This changes nothing.”

Lono almost laughed. The ocean’s roar filled the silence between them.

Malia stepped forward, close enough that Lono could see the determination in her eyes. “It changes everything.”

Then she walked toward the water, her board under her arm, the ring glinting like a challenge.

Chapter 13

The governor’s house in Kahala smelled of polished wood and old money. Not the money he’d made—no, that reeked of diesel and corruption—but the kind of money that hung in portraits and whispered from mahogany panels. Hokuokalani stood at the window, his hands braced on the sill, watching the North Shore glow under a sky gone blood-orange at dusk. The sunset bled into the ocean, the waves at Pipeline still riding high from the Legends Tour finale.

His phone buzzed against the wood. A text from his aide: *Journalists are asking about the WSL resignation. What do we tell them?*

Hokuokalani didn’t answer. He set the phone down.

Malia’s words still echoed in the chamber of his skull. *I choose the ocean.*

A car door slammed outside. Footsteps on the driveway. He didn’t turn. “You’re late.”

Sophie Laurent stepped into the room, her boots clicking on the hardwood. She wore a fitted blazer over a surfer’s rash guard, the Laurent logo stitched in gold across the chest. Her sunglasses came off. “I had to get Malia out of there before she did something we’d all regret.”

Hokuokalani turned. “She already did.”

Sophie exhaled through her nose. “She’s a child.”

“She’s a Hoopai,” he said. “And you’re a Laurent. Both of you playing at politics you don’t understand.” He poured two fingers of bourbon into a crystal glass. The ice clinked. “She handed him the ring. On live. On tape. You think that won’t play in Paris?”

Sophie’s fingers twitched at her side. “Malia was always going to defy you. That’s not new. What’s new is that she defied me too.”

Hokuokalani drank. The burn didn’t reach his head. “You still think this is about you?”

“It’s always been about me. Or did you forget whose family owns half the surf industry?” She stepped closer, her voice dropping. “I came here to warn you. Kai Ulu’s streaming a live special in thirty minutes. He’s calling it *The Reef of Kamehameha*. He’s using Lono’s royal tattoos, the journal fragments, the coup details—everything. It’s going to air globally.”

Hokuokalani laughed. It was a dry, humorless sound. “And you’re afraid of the truth?”

“I’m afraid of what it does to your deals.” She tapped her nails on the arm of a chair. “Private resorts. Exclusive breaks. A closed system where only the right bloodlines get in. You really think the world will accept that after Lono’s little circus today?”

He set the glass down hard enough to slosh bourbon over the rim. “I don’t care what the world accepts. I care about what they *pay*.”

Sophie’s smile was razor-thin. “Then you’re worse off than I thought. Because Kai’s got Malia in that stream. She’s talking about the royal journals. She’s saying the land never belonged to you to sell.” She leaned in. “And she’s wearing Lono’s ring.”

Hokuokalani’s jaw tightened. “She doesn’t know what she’s holding.”

“She knows enough to make investors nervous.” Sophie pulled out her phone. “I’ve got a private jet waiting. We leave in two hours. You can come with me, or you can drown here. Your choice.”

A knock at the door. His aide stepped in, face pale. “Sir, the WSL liaison is on line one. He says he can’t—”

Hokuokalani’s hand shot out. He grabbed the phone before the aide could finish. “What?”

The voice on the other end was strained, almost apologetic. “Governor, I’m calling to resign my position. Effective immediately. I won’t drown with you.”

The line went dead.

Sophie didn’t move. Her eyes stayed locked on Hokuokalani’s face as the seconds stretched. The bourbon dripped onto the carpet.

He set the phone down. His voice was quiet. “You were right. This isn’t about you.”

Outside, the sunset flared into darkness. The ocean’s roar filled the silence between them.

Sophie picked up her sunglasses. “I’ll be at the airport.”

Hokuokalani didn’t answer. He walked to the window again, hands in his pockets, watching the last light die over Pipeline. The waves were still there. Still perfect. Still his to lose.

Chapter 14

The Kapule Estate had been rebuilt from the ashes—stone by stone, beam by beam, but the land remembered what the sky forgot. Lono stood in the great hall, the restored bones of the old palace stretching around him. The council sat in a half-circle of wooden chairs carved with waves and makau, their faces unreadable in the afternoon light slanting through the lanai. Malia hovered near the back, arms crossed, her rash guard still damp from the surf. Kai leaned against a pillar, fingers tapping a restless rhythm against the wood.

Lono didn’t sit. He didn’t bow. He just looked at the high seat at the center of the room—the chair that should’ve been his father’s.

Councilor Nalani cleared her throat. “You’ve proven your blood, Lono Kapule. The journals, the line of descent, the will of the ancestors—” She paused. “We offer you the throne.”

The word sat heavy in the air. Lono swallowed. “I don’t want your crown.”

A murmur rippled through the council. Malia’s fingers tightened on her arms.

Councilor Keoni spoke next, voice measured. “Then what do you want?”

Lono turned to the lanai, where the North Shore stretched beyond in a glittering expanse of blue-green and white foam. “I want your breaks.”

Silence.

Nalani’s eyes narrowed. “You’d give away the sacred waves?”

“They were never yours to sell,” Lono said. “They’re the people’s. The ocean doesn’t belong to kings or governors or surf empires. It belongs to the ones who ride it.” He gestured to the open doors. “To the ones who remember how to listen.”

Keoni stood. “You’d dismantle everything we’ve built? The private contracts? The resort deals? The control?”

Lono smiled. “I’d return it.”

Malia shifted, her boots scuffing the floor. “And me?”

The room stilled. All eyes turned to her.

Lono didn’t look at Malia right away. He walked to the lanai instead, hands in the pockets of his board shorts, and stared out at Pipeline. The waves were unloading perfect barrels, the kind that made legends. He could see Sophie out there now, paddling into the lineup, her yellow-and-blue jersey sharp against the whitewater.

Then he turned.

Malia stood with her chin lifted, but her eyes weren’t defiant. They were waiting.

Lono walked to her. He pulled a second wetsuit from the bundle at his feet—neoprene still smelling of rubber and salt. Not his size. Not anyone’s size, just a spare, worn at the knees.

He held it out.

“Only if you earn it,” he said.

Malia didn’t take it. Not yet. “You think that’s enough? A wetsuit and a wave?”

Lono didn’t answer. He tossed the wetsuit onto a chair. It landed with a quiet slap.

Councilor Nalani’s voice cut through the tension. “Lono. The throne is more than a seat. It’s a legacy. A responsibility.”

Lono looked at the chair again. Then he crouched and picked up a handful of sand from the lanai’s floor. He let it sift through his fingers. “My father once told me that the sea doesn’t care about crowns. It only cares about how you ride.” He stood, brushing the sand from his palm. “So I choose the waves.”

Malia finally took the wetsuit. Held it against her chest. The council members exchanged glances, some indignant, some thoughtful.

Keoni sighed. “Then what do we call you?”

Lono looked at Kai, who gave him a slow nod. Then he looked at Malia. “Call me what I always was.” He stepped past her, toward the lanai, the ocean breeze tangling his hair. “A surfer.”

Malia followed. So did Kai.

No one in the council stopped them.

Outside, the afternoon sun glared off the water. The lineup at Pipeline was alive with riders—locals, legends, groms. Sophie caught the next set, her board lifting into a flawless turn. The crowd on the beach roared.

Lono didn’t hear it. Not really.

He was too busy unzipping his own wetsuit, peeling it off, standing there in a faded boardshort and bare feet. The sand burned, but he didn’t care.

Malia tossed her borrowed rash guard onto the sand. She unzipped the wetsuit and slipped it on. It swallowed her, but she rolled up the sleeves, folded the cuffs tight.

Kai grinned. “Guess that makes us rebels without a cause.”

Malia smirked. “Or rebels with the right one.”

Lono didn’t wait. He sprinted into the surf, leapt onto his board, and paddled out past the breakers. The others followed.

The North Shore welcomed them.

Chapter 15

Pipeline roared. The North Shore swallowed a twenty-foot Waimea Bowl and spat it out as a perfect tube, glass-clear and slow, curling like a lover’s invitation. Lono didn’t wait. He drove down the face, no flash, no spin, just the deep crouch that turned speed into prayer. The crowd screamed his name—*Ka-moana!*—but it wasn’t prayer. It was envy.

He rode the shoulder, let the lip feather over his head, and kicked out at the last second. The wave exploded behind him. Salt water stung his eyes. Tourists clutched each other’s arms. Sponsors with clipboards looked like they might faint.

Kai stood waist-deep in the channel, camera held high above the foam, shouting, “That’s a six-point-eight, judges! That’s a six-point-eight!”

Lono paddled back out. The next set approached. He took the first one on the shoulder, angled hard toward the inside, and let it jack him up. The board lifted his feet; the reef rose to meet him. He didn’t stall. He didn’t hesitate. He bottom-turned, drove off the tail, and launched himself into the air. The snap was clean. The board landed square. The crowd erupted.

Malia watched from the inside shoulder, arms crossed, a white Kapule cap pulled low. She hadn’t clapped yet.

Hokuokalani stood on the sand, high-visibility vest zipped over a salmon-colored Aloha shirt, hands clenched around a leaf blower he wasn’t using. His face was calm. His jaw was not.

Sophie Laurent stood at the edge of the crowd, sunglasses reflecting the wave, lips parted like she was about to speak French. Then she locked eyes with Lono. She smiled. Nodded once.

He nodded back.

Kai’s voice cut through the roar. “Lono, Sophie just offered to sell her entire French surf empire. She’s saying the money goes to Kapule scholarships. Global press is already calling it the biggest philanthropy in surf history.”

Lono didn’t look at Kai. He watched the horizon, the next set rising like a wall of blue-green glass.

“You okay?” Kai asked, splashing up beside him.

“Never better,” Lono said.

Malia pushed off the rail, waded into waist-deep water, and waited.

The set turned. Lono took the third wave. He rode it all the way to the channel, kicked out, and turned toward shore. The crowd parted. He walked up the beach, board tucked under his arm, water dripping from his trunks.

Hokuokalani didn’t move. He stared at the water like it had betrayed him.

Malia stepped forward. “You lied to me.”

Lono stopped. “I never lied.”

“You gave my ring away.”

“Borrowed,” he corrected. “I said I’d give it back when I was ready.”

She stepped closer. The wet fabric of her board shorts clung to her thighs. “You don’t get to decide when I’m ready.”

He looked at her. Not at her face. At the scar above her eyebrow—faded pink, from a wipeout at Haleiwa when she was twelve. He remembered it. She didn’t.

“Then take it back,” he said.

She didn’t.

Tourists shuffled around them, phones raised, jaws slack. The next set thundered in. The ocean didn’t wait.

Malia’s hand twitched at her side. “They’re saying you’re giving away my birthright.”

“No,” Lono said. “I’m giving it back.”

The words hung between them, heavier than the salt in the air.

Sophie closed the distance, her perfume cutting through the iodine and seaweed. “Lono, darling, we need to talk about the paperwork.”

Malia’s eyes flicked to Sophie. Then to Lono. “You two getting married?”

Lono didn’t answer.

Hokuokalani’s leaf blower sputtered to life. He didn’t point it. He just stood there, engine idling, watching the scene like it was a bad luau he couldn’t escape.

Kai shouldered his camera, zoomed in on the three of them. “This could be the cover of Surfing Magazine. ‘Love, Power, and a Perfect Barrel.’”

Malia turned on Kai. “Shut. It. Off.”

Kai lowered the lens.

The ocean hummed. The crowd buzzed. The board in Lono’s hand felt warm from the sun.

Sophie broke first. “We have champagne in the villa. Contracts on the table. Hawaii’s future in our hands.”

Malia stepped into Lono’s space. “You surf Pipeline at midnight tonight.”

He raised an eyebrow.

“Not for the crowd,” she said. “For me.”

The leaf blower died. Hokuokalani’s face flickered—something like pride, something like defeat.

The first wave of the next set broke white and heavy in the channel.

Lono walked toward it.

Malia followed.

Sophie stood still, sunglasses glinting, the French surf empire hanging in the balance like a wave she couldn’t quite catch.

Kai ran ahead, phone raised, livestream already counting viewers.

The sea kept coming.
